text: We present our experience in the laparoscopic treatment of an aneurysm of the splenic artery. This was a fifty-five year old woman who was being evaluated for kidney stones and she was incidentally diagnosed with splenic artery aneurysm. Her past medical history was significant for IBS and asthma as a child. During her pre-op evaluation, a CT scan of the abdomen showed a round structure consistent with aneurysm of the splenic artery, with calcifications where the presence of blood flow could not be excluded. Here, the splenic aneurysm can be observed in a coronal view. Arterogram of the splenic artery demonstrated a stop image in its mid-portion and its continuation through collateral pancreatic circulation, redirecting the flow towards the distal portion of the splenic artery.

The surgeon retracts the liver using a Nathanson liver retractor, opens the part flaccida, and exposes the retrocavity. A round structure, identified as the aneurysm, is visible on the superior edge of the pancreas. The aneurysm is dissected from the retroperitoneum using a harmonic scalpel, with dissection continuing towards the pancreas using blunt maneuvers. A collateral vessel from the aneurysm is transected.

The surgeon continues dissection towards the retroperitoneum, exposing the splenic artery proximal to the aneurysm. The splenic vein is visible during the procedure. A right angle instrument is used to complete the dissection, and a metallic vascular clamp is placed on the artery. The distal portion of the splenic artery is dissected using hook electrocautery, and Hemologs are applied to clip the artery. The spleen shows no signs of ischemia.

The surgeon transects the splenic artery with scissors and uses the harmonic scalpel to transect collateral vessels. The distal portion of the splenic artery is retracted to expose the aneurysm, which is further freed from the pancreatic tissue using the harmonic scalpel and blunt dissection. All visible vessels are clipped.

The surgeon completes the dissection, leaving the proximal portion of the splenic artery exposed. Hemolog clips are applied, and the vascular clamp is retrieved. The proximal splenic artery is transected using scissors, with no visible changes in the spleen's appearance. The specimen is placed in an endobag and retrieved through a trochanter incision.
